title: The Laya Process — Bhūta-śuddhi and the Absorption Hierarchy from Layayoga
subtitle: Goswami's exposition of laya as absorptive concentration: the definition of laya, apañcīkaraṇa, the two stages of samādhi, and the 1:4:2 prāṇāyāma ratio

summary.one_line: A compilation of Goswami's teaching on the laya (absorption) process from Layayoga: the definition and derivation of laya, the apañcīkaraṇa hierarchy (earth→water→fire→air→void→consciousness), the ...
summary.scope: Covers the etymological meaning of laya (√li = 'be absorbed in'), the Waidika and Tantrika forms of the absorption process, the apañcīkaraṇa sequence, the two stages of laya (sensory absorption and superconscious absorption), and the prāṇāyāma ratio 1-4-2 (16-64-32) as the breath-control foundation.

sources.label: Goswami, Shyam Sundar — Layayoga (1980/1999), Layayoga — Its Significance and Method, pp. 69–78

body.text: By the process of control, the cosmic principles — conative faculties, senses, sense-mind, sense-consciousness and superconsciousness — become unmanifested and are absorbed into the spiritual Power-Consciousness in deep concentration. The absorption of the cosmic principles in deep concentration, which is associated with control, is laya. 'The term laya is derived from √li meaning "be absorbed in." Laya, in the technical sense of yoga, means absorption in deep concentration. When the multiforms of consciousness undergo absorption in concentration and do not arise again to interrupt it, concentration develops into samādhi' (Layayoga, p. 69, citing Adhyatmopaniṣad 42). Laya has two stages: first, the absorption of sense-consciousness; second, the complete absorption of superconsciousness into Supreme Power-Consciousness. 'Laya develops into samādhi' (ibid., p. 70).

The process of absorption, technically termed apañcīkaraṇa ('dis-quintuplication'), is described as follows: the 'earth'-form is absorbed in the 'water'-form (in concentration); 'water'-form in 'fire'-form; 'fire'-form in 'air'-form; 'air'-form in 'void'-form; 'void'-form in I-consciousness (which includes sense-consciousness, intellect, will-mind and sense-mind); I-consciousness in the mahat-principle (superconsciousness); mahat in avyakta (prakṛti, primus); and avyakta in puruṣa (disembodied consciousness principle). This sequence is drawn from the Paiṅgalopaniṣad (3.6). It is a ladder of absorption from the densest to the subtlest, each stage being the 'heart' of the next — earth is the heart of smell, water of taste, fire of sight, air of touch, void of sound (Layayoga, pp. 72-73, citing Subalopaniṣad 7.13.2).

When concentration becomes deepest, samādhi is attained. Samādhi in the superconscious field is termed samprajñāta samādhi (superconscious concentration). The control power has now reached its highest degree, termed nirodha or saṃnirodha (supercontrol). This is the limit of mental control. Then samādhi consciousness is completely absorbed into the power aspect of Supreme Consciousness — this is the stage of asamprajñāta samādhi (non-mens concentration). In the Waidika laya process, the conative faculties (speech, prehension, locomotion, excretion, reproduction) are controlled stage by stage, followed by the senses (smell, taste, sight, touch, sound). Sense-consciousness becomes free from objective images (pratyāhāra), leading to dhāraṇā, dhyāna, and finally samprajñāta samādhi. The Kathopaniṣad (1.3.13) provides the scriptural basis: 'Speech should be controlled in manas, manas in jñāna-ātman, jñāna-ātman in mahat, and mahat in Supreme Ātman' (Layayoga, pp. 69-70).

The breath-control process foundational to laya uses the ratio 1:4:2 for inspiration, breath-suspension, and expiration. The actual measures are 16-64-32 counts. 'Inspire through the left nostril slowly, counting 16 and at the same time doing the sound-process (japa) with "A" of the praṇava and with concentration; then suspend the breath for 64 measures and at the same time make the sound-process with "U" of the praṇava; and finally expire slowly through the right nostril counting 32 with the sound-process of "M"' (Darshanopaniṣad, 6.3-6, cited at Layayoga, p. 60). The 1:4:2 ratio binds the three respiratory acts to the three syllables of OṂ (A-U-M), making prāṇāyāma itself a mantra practice. This is the sahita (inspiratory-expiratory-suspension) form of prāṇāyāma, which precedes kewala (non-inspiratory-non-expiratory-suspension) (Layayoga, pp. 60-61).

In the Waidika process of absorption, termed 'Ekadhanāvarodhana' (Prāṇa-Control), the five sensory forms are reduced to their mantra-forms linked with appropriate deities. With the help of mantras, the senses are absorbed in concentration in the deities; the deities are then dissolved in the central deity in the form of Kuṇḍalinī, aroused by the absorption of prāṇic forces in prāṇāyāma. After this, sense-consciousness and finally superconsciousness are absorbed into Kuṇḍalinī. The process is governed by the principle: 'From mind void-form, from void-form air-form, from air-form fire-form, from that water-form and from that earth-form, and from that in turn the entire universe and all beings have been manifested' (Sannyasopaniṣad, 4.6, cited at Layayoga, p. 72). The absorption proceeds in reverse order of manifestation, coiling the cosmos back into its source.

In the Tantrika form of layayoga, Kuṇḍalinī is the Supreme Power in her spiritual aspect. The method of arousing Kuṇḍalinī and uniting her with Parama Śiva — Supreme Spirit — is the essential part. First, the aroused Kuṇḍalinī moves towards the Supreme Spirit, creating a spiritual flow stage by stage, during which the various cosmic principles are absorbed into Kuṇḍalinī. Then the spiritual consciousness itself is absorbed into Kuṇḍalinī, and ultimately Kuṇḍalinī herself is absorbed into Supreme Spirit. That form of concentration in which consciousness is completely free from non-spiritual elements by absorption, and is fully illuminated by the luminous Kuṇḍalinī, is the highest stage of samprajñāta samādhi. Thereafter Kuṇḍalinī absorbs the spiritual consciousness and finally she herself is absorbed into Supreme Spirit — asamprajñāta samādhi (Layayoga, pp. 74-75).

The various principles to be absorbed in concentration by stages are enumerated from the Praśnopaniṣad (4.8): the earth, water, fire, air and void principles; subtle earth, water, fire, air and void principles; the principles of smell and its objects, of taste and its objects, of sight and its objects, of touch and its objects, and of hearing and its objects; the conative principles — reproduction, excretion, locomotion, prehension and speech; sense-mind and its functions; intellect, I-ness and sense-consciousness and their functions; supermind (mahat) and its function; and the creative aspect of infinite energy — all these are to be absorbed (Layayoga, p. 70). When all principles are absorbed in concentration, what remains is Brahman — Supreme Spirit. 'That (Brahman) is infinite, being by itself, beyond mind, subtler than the subtlest, far away and still near; that Brahman is hidden in what has been manifested as life-mind-matter' (Muṇḍakopaniṣad, 3.1.7, cited at Layayoga, p. 71).

The Waidika laya process is scripturally grounded in the Kathopaniṣad (1.3.13): 'Speech should be controlled in manas, manas in jñāna-ātman, jñāna-ātman in mahan, and mahan in Supreme Ātman.' Here speech stands for all organs of voluntary action and the senses. The organs of action are controlled in manas (will-mind); the senses are controlled in manas (sense-mind). Jñāna-ātman is sense-consciousness and intellect, and mahan is the I-less supermind (superconsciousness). By the power of control manifested in concentration, the senses and the sensory aspects of the mind are absorbed. After this, supermind is manifested and concentration develops into samādhi. Finally, the supermind is absorbed in supreme concentration into Supreme Spirit — the state of non-mens concentration (asamprajñāta samādhi). This is the ancient Waidika path of absorption (Layayoga, pp. 69-70).

In the Waidika laya process, the conative faculties (karmendriyas) — namely speech, prehension, locomotion, excretion and reproduction — are to be controlled by the will-mind in concentration, stage by stage. These actions become unmanifested owing to their tranquillization by control. The stages of control are as follows: (1) reproduction, (2) excretion, (3) locomotion, (4) prehension, and (5) speech. These control processes are carried out in the lower five subtle centres. In this way the body becomes motionless and the mind becomes free of all thoughts about the body. Thereafter the senses — smell, taste, sight, touch, and sound — are also controlled in the lower five subtle centres, while the sense-mind, residing in the sixth subtle centre, is also controlled. Consequently the sense-mind does not radiate object-substance to sense-consciousness, resulting in pratyāhāra (sensory control) (Layayoga, pp. 69-70).

The Yogatattwopaniṣad (23-24) defines layayoga as follows, as cited by Goswami: 'Layayoga is that in which chitta (sense-consciousness) undergoes laya, that is, becomes absorbed in deep concentration; there are many methods for achieving this: but the most effective is dhyana (deep concentration) on God in form, which can be done also while walking, standing, eating, and resting. This is layayoga.' Sense-consciousness, which is the field where sensory images are constantly formed, becomes transformed through concentration into a state free from image-undulations — superconsciousness. This transformation is achieved by taking the divine form as the object of concentration, made living through Iṣṭadevatā who appears from the living mantra (Layayoga, p. 68).

When the senses and sense-mind become inoperative, sense-consciousness becomes masked. But in layayoga concentration, sense-consciousness is not merely suppressed — it is absorbed, together with sense-mind, will-mind and intellect, and the undifferentiated conscious 'substance' of sense-consciousness is spiritually transformed into highly rarefied superconsciousness. This is the stage of superconscious concentration. The absorption of sense-consciousness is the principal part of layayoga, as the attainment of samādhi entirely depends on it. Aditya said: 'The real chittakshaya (complete absorption of sense-consciousness), which is superconscious in nature, is yoga' (Akshyupaniṣad, 2.3). The absorption process (apañcīkaraṇa, 'dis-quintuplication') proceeds as follows: earth-form in water-form, water-form in fire-form, fire-form in air-form, air-form in void-form, void-form in I-consciousness (including sense-consciousness, intellect, will-mind and sense-mind), I-consciousness in the mahat-principle (superconsciousness), mahat in avyakta (prakṛti), and avyakta in puruṣa (Paingalopaniṣad, 3.6). Thus consciousness is traced back to its source (Layayoga, pp. 71-73).

There is a Waidika process of absorption termed Ekadhanāvarodhana (Prāṇa-Control), from the Kauṣītakyupaniṣad (2.2). The sensory functions of the mind are based on the functioning of bio-energy in the body, and the concentrated state of consciousness is difficult to attain without the control of bio-energy. First, the five sensory forms are reduced to their right mantra-forms with which are linked appropriate deities. With the help of the mantras, the senses are absorbed in concentration in the deities. Thereafter the deities are dissolved in the central deity in the form of Kuṇḍalinī, aroused by the absorption of prāṇic forces in prāṇāyāma while in concentration. After this, sense-consciousness and finally superconsciousness are absorbed into Kuṇḍalinī. By controlling the prāṇa-apāna bio-forces, spiritual light is ignited in the mind in deep concentration, leading to the attainment of Brahman (Sannyasopaniṣad, 4.6, cited at Layayoga, p. 72).

gaps: Bhūta-śuddhi as a distinct ritual practice not extracted
Laya in relation to mantra-yoga and nāda-yoga not covered

issues.title: The threefold laya (Waidika/Tantrika/Pouranika) distinctions need further detail
issues.description: Goswami sketches three forms but treats primarily Waidika and Tantrika; Pouranika absorption process is fragmentary.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Layayoga laya process section. Seven passages covering definition, apañcīkaraṇa hierarchy, two samādhi stages, 1:4:2 ratio, Waidika and Tantrika forms, and the full absorption list.

Added 5 new passages: Waidika laya from Kathopaniṣad, the five conative faculties and their control sequence, the definition of laya from Yogatattwopaniṣad, sense-consciousness transformation and the apañcīkaraṇa hierarchy, and the Ekadhanāvarodhana (prāṇa-control) method.

timeline.event: Updated to v0.2.0 — added 5 passages on Kathopaniṣad laya, conative faculties, Yogatattwopaniṣad definition, sense-consciousness transformation, and Ekadhanāvarodhana
